title: ACLS – Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia
description: A monitored in-hospital patient develops pulseless ventricular tachycardia. Practise recognising pulseless VTach, applying the shockable ACLS algorithm, and differentiating it from stable VTach.
learningObjectives: Distinguish pulseless VTach from stable (perfusing) VTach
Apply the ACLS shockable rhythm algorithm for pulseless VTach
Recognise wide-complex regular tachycardia on the ECG
Administer epinephrine and amiodarone at appropriate intervals

states.prompt: A 62-year-old male with ischaemic cardiomyopathy (EF 25%) is on telemetry post-MI day 2. The alarm sounds – the monitor shows a REGULAR wide-complex tachycardia at 200 bpm. He is unresponsive when you try to rouse him. No carotid pulse is detected after a 5-second check. Which ACLS algorithm applies?

options.label: Shockable cardiac arrest algorithm (same as VFib)

explanations: Correct! Pulseless VTach is treated identically to VFib: unsynchronised defibrillation + CPR + vasopressors + antiarrhythmics.
Synchronised cardioversion is for patients with a pulse. This patient is pulseless – treat as cardiac arrest.
Heart rate is 200 bpm – bradycardia does not apply.
A pulseless patient requires immediate resuscitation; observation is inappropriate.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies pulseless VTach as a shockable cardiac arrest

states.prompt: Which ECG feature BEST distinguishes VTach from SVT with aberrant conduction (a key differential)?

options.label: Presence of fusion beats or atrioventricular dissociation (AV dissociation)
Heart rate > 150 bpm (rate alone differentiates VT from SVT)
QRS duration ≥ 100 ms is sufficient to diagnose VTach

explanations: Correct! AV dissociation (P-waves independent of QRS), capture beats, and fusion beats are pathognomonic of VTach.
Rate alone cannot distinguish VT from SVT – both can exceed 150 bpm.
QRS ≥ 120 ms (not 100 ms) suggests aberrancy; but SVT with bundle-branch block also has wide QRS. AV dissociation is the key.
Both VT and SVT with aberrancy can be regular; regularity alone does not diagnose VT.

checklistItems.description: Identifies AV dissociation as pathognomonic of VTach

states.prompt: CPR is underway. Pads attached. Ready to defibrillate. Should the shock be SYNCHRONISED or UNSYNCHRONISED for pulseless VTach?

options.label: Either is acceptable for pulseless VTach

explanations: Correct! Pulseless VTach is treated with UNSYNCHRONISED defibrillation (same as VFib). Synchronisation is only for patients with a pulse.
Synchronised cardioversion risks triggering VFib if the shock falls on the T-wave in a pulseless rhythm. Use defibrillation mode.
For pulseless VTach, only unsynchronised (defibrillation) mode is appropriate.
Defibrillation takes priority over pharmacological therapy in shockable rhythms.

checklistItems.description: Correctly selects unsynchronised defibrillation for pulseless VTach

states.prompt: After two shocks and epinephrine, VTach persists. You consider reversible causes. This patient had an acute MI. Which '5H/5T' cause is MOST likely driving refractory VTach in this context?
explanations: Correct! In post-MI patients, refractory VTach/VFib is most commonly driven by ongoing coronary ischaemia. Emergent catheterisation may be warranted post-ROSC.
Hyperkalaemia is an important cause but less likely in an isolated post-MI setting without renal failure or metabolic disturbance.
Tension pneumothorax is a 5T cause but unlikely in a step-down patient without recent intervention.
Hypoxia is critical to address but is less specific to this post-MI context than coronary thrombosis.

checklistItems.description: Identifies coronary ischaemia as most likely reversible cause

states.prompt: VTach continues. Third shock delivered. Which antiarrhythmic is administered now and at what dose?
explanations: Correct! Amiodarone 300 mg IV is first-line for shock-refractory VTach/VFib. A second dose of 150 mg can be given if needed.
Correct! Lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV is an acceptable alternative when amiodarone is unavailable.
Magnesium is indicated for Torsades de Pointes, not monomorphic VTach.
Adenosine terminates AV-node-dependent SVT — it has no effect on VTach.

checklistItems.description: Administers amiodarone or lidocaine for shock-refractory VTach
checklistItems.evaluationHint: Patient selected drug_amio or drug_lido option

states.prompt: ROSC achieved after the fourth shock. The patient remains unconscious. 12-lead ECG shows STEMI (anterior). What is the next priority?

options.label: Urgent cardiology consultation and emergent PCI regardless of neurological status
Wait for neurological recovery before PCI
Give fibrinolytic therapy since PCI is risky post-arrest
Start amiodarone infusion and defer cardiac workup to morning

explanations: Correct! AHA/ESC guidelines recommend emergent coronary angiography (and PCI if indicated) post-ROSC with STEMI, irrespective of comatose status.
Neurological status should not delay PCI — unconsciousness alone is not a contraindication to emergent PCI post-ROSC.
PCI is preferred over fibrinolysis post-cardiac arrest due to bleeding risk and lower PCI morbidity.
Amiodarone infusion is useful for arrhythmia prophylaxis, but STEMI management takes priority.

checklistItems.description: Correctly prioritises emergent PCI after ROSC with STEMI

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• Pulseless VTach = shockable; treat with unsynchronised defibrillation
• CPR immediately after every shock; epinephrine q3–5 min
• Amiodarone 300 mg after 3rd shock for refractory VTach
• Post-ROSC STEMI → emergent PCI regardless of neurological status
